does: This survey synthesizes recent advances in federated learning approaches for training large language models in decentralized settings. It systematically reviews architectural innovations designed for distributed LLM training, analyzes performance optimization techniques that manage communication costs and computational efficiency, and catalogs security mechanisms addressing privacy threats. Notably, the work gives substantial treatment to machine unlearning—methods for systematically removing specific data contributions from trained models to satisfy regulatory obligations like the Right to be Forgotten. The survey evaluates perturbation-based unlearning approaches, model decomposition strategies, and incremental retraining techniques, assessing their trade-offs across efficiency, privacy guarantees, and model utility through case studies and empirical comparisons in realistic federated scenarios.
connects: This work builds on decades of distributed machine learning research and more recent federated learning literature pioneering privacy-preserving collaborative training. It directly extends foundational FL frameworks developed for smaller models by addressing the specific challenges posed by LLMs—their scale, the complexity of decentralized training, and heightened privacy risks from memorization. The survey also engages with emerging machine unlearning literature that arose from GDPR and similar regulations demanding rights to erasure, connecting privacy law with technical ML capability. It synthesizes across multiple research threads: secure aggregation and differential privacy mechanisms from FL security work, architectural adaptations from distributed deep learning, and unlearning methods from recent work on model modification and data deletion compliance.
enables: This comprehensive treatment of FL-driven LLMs positions future work toward production-ready federated language model systems serving privacy-sensitive applications across healthcare, finance, and government. By identifying and evaluating unlearning strategies, it enables compliant deployment of LLMs across jurisdictions with strong data protection regulations. The analysis of architectural and performance trade-offs creates a foundation for designing next-generation federated systems with better scaling properties. Future research can build on this survey to develop hybrid approaches combining multiple unlearning techniques, create stronger privacy-efficiency trade-off bounds, and design LLM-specific aggregation protocols. The work also points toward exploring federated fine-tuning of foundation models as a practical alternative to centralized training, and toward understanding theoretical limits of privacy, utility, and computational cost in decentralized language model training.
